revision.text:
[[sn:commons:Category:Outdoor hot spring baths]][[ja:露天風呂]][[ko:노천탕]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10856214]] Wikidata name: outdoor hot spring bath [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1={{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}} refers to communal baths outside lodging facilities shared by multiple users, {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} denotes in-house hot spring baths privately installed within lodging facilities, and {{ill|Rotenburo|ja|露天風呂|1=outdoor hot spring bath|1=Rotenburo|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rotenburo|13=User:Immanuelle/Rotenburo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10856214}} describes open-air baths typically without a roof or full enclosure.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:41, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Onsen in Nachikatsuura, Japan.jpg|thumb|280px|An open-air bath overlooking the sea ([[:en:Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen]])]] [[File:Iwojima Higashi onsen.jpg|280px|thumb|A self-flowing hot spring “{{ill|Higashi Onsen (Iōjima)|ja|東温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Onsen (Iōjima)|1=Higashi Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527997}}” facing the East China Sea ([[:en:Iōjima (Kagoshima)|Satsuma Iōjima]])]] '''Rotenburo''' (open-air bath, 露天風呂) is a type of [[:en:Furo|Bath]] built outdoors with no roof or enclosure[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%9C%B2%E5%A4%A9%E9%A2%A8%E5%91%82-663841 Kotobank - Digital Daijisen (Sanseidō)]. It is also called '''Noburo''' (野風呂) or '''Notenburo''' (野天風呂)When referred to as 野天風呂 (or 野風呂), it may mean a bath that is not entirely in its natural state like a {{ill|Wild hot spring|ja|野湯|1=Wild Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild Hot Springs|qq=}} but lacks as much development in walls or roofing as a typical open-air bath.. == Overview == Open-air baths can be considered the original “landscape” of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] bathing. They began as naturally emerging hot springs like {{ill|Wild hot spring|ja|野湯|1=Wild Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild Hot Springs|qq=}}, in which a simple bathing area (bathtub) was arranged. Rotenburo (open-air baths) are highly popular at hot spring resorts. Reasons include being able to enjoy scenic views while bathing, experiencing a sense of openness, and having less risk of overheating (since heat does not accumulate as much as in an indoor setting). As a result, many [[:en:Ryokan|Ryokan]] and {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} offer open-air baths in addition to {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}}. In recent years, not only traditional onsen but also many {{ill|Supersento|ja|スーパー銭湯|1=Super Sentō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Supersento|13=User:Immanuelle/Super Sentō|qq=}} have installed open-air baths. Up through the early 1980s, indoor styles such as {{ill|Roman bath|ja|ローマ風呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Roman bath|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579581}} or {{ill|Jungle bath|ja|ジャングル風呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jungle bath|qq=}} were mainstream. However, around the late 1980s during the [[:en:Japanese asset price bubble|Japanese asset price bubble]], rotenburo began to draw more attention. Sometimes, even a bath that has a ceiling and three walls (thus feeling nearly as enclosed as an indoor bath) might be marketed as a “rotenburo,” or described as “semi-open-air” (“半露天”). == Rotenburo Banzuke == '''Rotenburo Banzuke''' (露天風呂番付) is a form of {{ill|Onsen banzuke|ja|温泉番付|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen banzuke|1=onsen banzuke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11562960}}, Ranking hot spring areas that are famous for their open-air baths. It was compiled in 1981, edited by '''Noguchi Fuyuto'''. In this Ranking, the Western {{ill|Yokozuna|ja|横綱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokozuna|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=yokozuna|WD=Q1067277}} is the “Sunayu” at {{ill|Yubara Onsen|ja|湯原温泉|1=Yubara Onsen|2=ja|3=湯原温泉|4=ceb|5=Yubara hotspring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563486}}, and the Eastern Yokozuna is {{ill|Takaragawa Onsen|ja|宝川温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaragawa Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水上温泉郷|1=Minakami Onsenkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548229}}. At Yubara Onsen, there is a large monument depicting this Rotenburo Banzuke, serving as a symbol of the hot spring resort. == Variations of Open-Air Baths == Yumura onsen20n4592.jpg|Open-air bath in a forest setting
([[:en:Yumura Onsen (Hyogo)|Yumura Onsen]]) Ryokukeitouen Kirishima City Kagoshima Pref09n4592.jpg|Open-air bath at a mountain stream
({{ill|Sakaenoo Onsen|ja|栄之尾温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaenoo Onsen|qq=}}) 190714 Settuskyo Onsen Takatsuki Osaka pref Japan01s3.jpg|Open-air bath with a gorge view
({{ill|Settsukyō Onsen|ja|摂津峡温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Settsukyō Onsen|qq=}}) Alpa Komakusa 2008.jpg|Open-air bath with a lake view (Tazawako Kōgen Onsen “Alpa Komakusa”) Tamatsukuri onsen yado01s3648.jpg|A guest room with an attached open-air bath
([[:en:Tamatsukuri Onsen|Tamatsukuri Onsen]]) Koeda-Fudousan Katsura Koeda P6237036.JPG|A personal open-air bath built on private villa grounds == Management == [[File:新穂高の湯001.jpg|thumb|Cleaning an open-air bath
({{ill|Shinhodaka Onsen|ja|新穂高温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinhodaka Onsen|1=Shinhotaka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11503887}})]] From a management perspective, especially with a free-flowing hot spring of high source temperature, weather changes can significantly alter the bath’s temperature, requiring more precise temperature control. Additionally, due to the constant possibility of contamination by [[:en:Legionella|Legionella]] bacteria, stricter hygiene measures than those for indoor baths are neededJapan Association of Living and Environmental Health “[http://www.seiei.or.jp/db-cho/le-11.html Legionella Prevention Guidelines]” (Revised March 1999; accessed January 5, 2016).. == Other == [[File:Iceland - Blue Lagoon 09 (6571266721).jpg|thumb|[[:en:Blue Lagoon (geothermal spa)|Blue Lagoon]] ([[:en:Iceland|Iceland]])]] * The open-air bath “Edo-jōwa Yoichi” at [[:en:Spa Resort Hawaiians|Spa Resort Hawaiians]] in [[:en:Iwaki, Fukushima|Iwaki, Fukushima]], [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]] is listed in the “[[:en:Guinness World Records|Guinness World Records]] 2009” as the world’s largest open-air bath with a total tub area of 1000m²{{Cite web|和書|url=http://blog.hawaiians.co.jp/archives/50736632.html|title=世界一の大露天風呂|publisher=スパリゾートハワイアンズ|date=2008-11-08|accessdate=2021-06-03}}. * In [[:en:Iceland|Iceland]], there is an open-air spa called the [[:en:Blue Lagoon (geothermal spa)|Blue Lagoon]] that covers about 5,000m² (with approximately 2,400m² open for general bathing)Iceland Tourism & Culture Research Institute “[http://www.iceland-kankobunka.jp/guide/reykjanes/blgn1.htm Tourist Spot Guide: Blue Lagoon]” (accessed January 5, 2016).. * [[:en:June 26|June 26]] is “Open-Air Bath Day” (a pun on “Ro (6) Ten (.) Bu (2) Ro (6)”). It was established on June 26, 1987, by {{ill|Yubara Onsen|ja|湯原温泉|1=Yubara Onsen|2=ja|3=湯原温泉|4=ceb|5=Yubara hotspring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563486}} in [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]Conceived in 1987 as part of a local revitalization project by young people in Yubara Onsen.[http://www.net626.co.jp/roten/index.html Official site of the 6.26 Open-Air Bath Day Committee] (Yubara Town Ryokan Cooperative). == Notes == == Sources == == See Also == * {{ill|part_of voyeurism in open-air baths incidents|ja|露天風呂|1=Rotenburo|1=Rotenburoでの組織的盗撮事件|1=Organized voyeurism case in an open-air bath|3=露天風呂での組織的盗撮事件|1=Organized Voyeurism Scandal at Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of voyeurism in open-air baths incidents|13=User:Immanuelle/Rotenburo|qq=}} == External links == * [http://www.net626.co.jp/banduke.htm Open-Air Bath Banzuke Chart] {{Onsen-stub}} '''Rotenburo''' (露天風呂) {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|露天風呂|version=102106783|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:Bathing]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[ja:露天風呂]] [[ja:%E9%9C%B2%E5%A4%A9%E9%A2%A8%E5%91%82]] [[ko:%EB%85%B8%EC%B2%9C%ED%83%95]] [[Category:Hot_springs_of_Japan]] {{Commons category|Outdoor hot spring baths}} [[ko:노천탕]] {{wikidata link|Q10856214}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

They began as naturally emerging hot springs like {{ill|Wild hot spring|ja|野湯|1=Wild Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild Hot Springs|qq=}}, in which a simple bathing area (bathtub) was arranged. Rotenburo (open-air baths) are highly popular at hot spring resorts. Reasons include being able to enjoy scenic views while bathing, experiencing a sense of openness, and having less risk of overheating (since heat does not accumulate as much as in an indoor setting). As a result, many [[:en:Ryokan|Ryokan]] and {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} offer open-air baths in addition to {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}}. Up through the early 1980s, indoor styles such as {{ill|Roman bath|ja|ローマ風呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Roman bath|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579581}} or {{ill|Jungle bath|ja|ジャングル風呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jungle bath|qq=}} were mainstream. However, around the late 1980s during the [[:en:Japanese asset price bubble|Japanese asset price bubble]], rotenburo began to draw more attention. Sometimes, even a bath that has a ceiling and three walls (thus feeling nearly as enclosed as an indoor bath) might be marketed as a “rotenburo,” or described as “semi-open-air” (“半露天”). == Rotenburo Banzuke == '''Rotenburo Banzuke''' (露天風呂番付) is a form of {{ill|Onsen banzuke|ja|温泉番付|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen banzuke|1=onsen banzuke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11562960}}, Ranking hot spring areas that are famous for their open-air baths. It was compiled in 1981, edited by '''Noguchi Fuyuto'''. In this Ranking, the Western {{ill|Yokozuna|ja|横綱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokozuna|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=yokozuna|WD=Q1067277}} is the “Sunayu” at {{ill|Yubara Onsen|ja|湯原温泉|1=Yubara Onsen|2=ja|3=湯原温泉|4=ceb|5=Yubara hotspring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563486}}, and the Eastern Yokozuna is {{ill|Takaragawa Onsen|ja|宝川温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaragawa Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水上温泉郷|1=Minakami Onsenkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548229}}. At Yubara Onsen, there is a large monument depicting this Rotenburo Banzuke, serving as a symbol of the hot spring resort. == Variations of Open-Air Baths == Yumura onsen20n4592.jpg|Open-air bath in a forest setting
([[:en:Yumura Onsen (Hyogo)|Yumura Onsen]]) Ryokukeitouen Kirishima City Kagoshima Pref09n4592.jpg|Open-air bath at a mountain stream
({{ill|Sakaenoo Onsen|ja|栄之尾温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaenoo Onsen|qq=}}) 190714 Settuskyo Onsen Takatsuki Osaka pref Japan01s3.jpg|Open-air bath with a gorge view
({{ill|Settsukyō Onsen|ja|摂津峡温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Settsukyō Onsen|qq=}}) Alpa Komakusa 2008.jpg|Open-air bath with a lake view (Tazawako Kōgen Onsen “Alpa Komakusa”) Tamatsukuri onsen yado01s3648.jpg|A guest room with an attached open-air bath
([[:en:Tamatsukuri Onsen|Tamatsukuri Onsen]]) Koeda-Fudousan Katsura Koeda P6237036.JPG|A personal open-air bath built on private villa grounds == Management == [[File:新穂高の湯001.jpg|thumb|Cleaning an open-air bath
({{ill|Shinhodaka Onsen|ja|新穂高温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinhodaka Onsen|1=Shinhotaka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11503887}})]] From a management perspective, especially with a free-flowing hot spring of high source temperature, weather changes can significantly alter the bath’s temperature, requiring more precise temperature control. Bu (2) Ro (6)”). It was established on June 26, 1987, by {{ill|Yubara Onsen|ja|湯原温泉|1=Yubara Onsen|2=ja|3=湯原温泉|4=ceb|5=Yubara hotspring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563486}} in [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]Conceived in 1987 as part of a local revitalization project by young people in Yubara Onsen.[http://www.net626.co.jp/roten/index.html Official site of the 6.26 Open-Air Bath Day Committee] (Yubara Town Ryokan Cooperative). == Notes == == Sources == == See Also == * {{ill|part_of voyeurism in open-air baths incidents|ja|露天風呂|1=Rotenburo|1=Rotenburoでの組織的盗撮事件|1=Organized voyeurism case in an open-air bath|3=露天風呂での組織的盗撮事件|1=Organized Voyeurism Scandal at Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of voyeurism in open-air baths incidents|13=User:Immanuelle/Rotenburo|qq=}} == External links == * [http://www.net626.co.jp/banduke.htm Open-Air Bath Banzuke Chart] {{Onsen-stub}} '''Rotenburo''' (露天風呂) {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|露天風呂|version=102106783|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:Bathing]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[ja:露天風呂]] [[ja:%E9%9C%B2%E5%A4%A9%E9%A2%A8%E5%91%82]] [[ko:%EB%85%B8%EC%B2%9C%ED%83%95]] [[Category:Hot_springs_of_Japan]] {{Commons category|Outdoor hot spring baths}} [[ko:노천탕]] {{wikidata link|Q10856214}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

[[commons:Category:Outdoor hot spring baths]][[ja:露天風呂]][[ko:노천탕]] [[commons:Category:Outdoor hot spring baths]][[ja:露天風呂]][[ko:노천탕]] [[sn:commons:Category:Outdoor hot spring baths]][[ja:露天風呂]][[ko:노천탕]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10856214]] Wikidata name: outdoor hot spring bath [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1={{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}} refers to communal baths outside lodging facilities shared by multiple users, {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} denotes in-house hot spring baths privately installed within lodging facilities, and {{ill|Rotenburo|ja|露天風呂|1=outdoor hot spring bath|1=Rotenburo|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rotenburo|13=User:Immanuelle/Rotenburo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10856214}} describes open-air baths typically without a roof or full enclosure.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:41, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Onsen in Nachikatsuura, Japan.jpg|thumb|280px|An open-air bath overlooking the sea ([[:en:Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen]])]] [[File:Iwojima Higashi onsen.jpg|280px|thumb|A self-flowing hot spring “{{ill|Higashi Onsen (Iōjima)|ja|東温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Onsen (Iōjima)|1=Higashi Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527997}}” facing the East China Sea ([[:en:Iōjima (Kagoshima)|Satsuma Iōjima]])]] '''Rotenburo''' (open-air bath, 露天風呂) is a type of [[:en:Furo|Bath]] built outdoors with no roof or enclosure[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%9C%B2%E5%A4%A9%E9%A2%A8%E5%91%82-663841 Kotobank - Digital Daijisen (Sanseidō)]. It is also called '''Noburo''' (野風呂) or '''Notenburo''' (野天風呂)When referred to as 野天風呂 (or 野風呂), it may mean a bath that is not entirely in its natural state like a {{ill|Wild hot spring|ja|野湯|1=Wild Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild Hot Springs|qq=}} but lacks as much development in walls or roofing as a typical open-air bath.. == Overview == Open-air baths can be considered the original “landscape” of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] bathing. They began as naturally emerging hot springs like {{ill|Wild hot spring|ja|野湯|1=Wild Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild Hot Springs|qq=}}, in which a simple bathing area (bathtub) was arranged. Rotenburo (open-air baths) are highly popular at hot spring resorts. Reasons include being able to enjoy scenic views while bathing, experiencing a sense of openness, and having less risk of overheating (since heat does not accumulate as much as in an indoor setting). As a result, many [[:en:Ryokan|Ryokan]] and {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} offer open-air baths in addition to {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}}. In recent years, not only traditional onsen but also many {{ill|Supersento|ja|スーパー銭湯|1=Super Sentō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Supersento|13=User:Immanuelle/Super Sentō|qq=}} have installed open-air baths. Up through the early 1980s, indoor styles such as {{ill|Roman bath|ja|ローマ風呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Roman bath|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579581}} or {{ill|Jungle bath|ja|ジャングル風呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jungle bath|qq=}} were mainstream. However, around the late 1980s during the [[:en:Japanese asset price bubble|Japanese asset price bubble]], rotenburo began to draw more attention. Sometimes, even a bath that has a ceiling and three walls (thus feeling nearly as enclosed as an indoor bath) might be marketed as a “rotenburo,” or described as “semi-open-air” (“半露天”). == Rotenburo Banzuke == '''Rotenburo Banzuke''' (露天風呂番付) is a form of {{ill|Onsen banzuke|ja|温泉番付|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen banzuke|1=onsen banzuke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11562960}}, Ranking hot spring areas that are famous for their open-air baths. It was compiled in 1981, edited by '''Noguchi Fuyuto'''. In this Ranking, the Western {{ill|Yokozuna|ja|横綱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokozuna|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=yokozuna|WD=Q1067277}} is the “Sunayu” at {{ill|Yubara Onsen|ja|湯原温泉|1=Yubara Onsen|2=ja|3=湯原温泉|4=ceb|5=Yubara hotspring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563486}}, and the Eastern Yokozuna is {{ill|Takaragawa Onsen|ja|宝川温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaragawa Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水上温泉郷|1=Minakami Onsenkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548229}}. At Yubara Onsen, there is a large monument depicting this Rotenburo Banzuke, serving as a symbol of the hot spring resort. == Variations of Open-Air Baths == Yumura onsen20n4592.jpg|Open-air bath in a forest setting
([[:en:Yumura Onsen (Hyogo)|Yumura Onsen]]) Ryokukeitouen Kirishima City Kagoshima Pref09n4592.jpg|Open-air bath at a mountain stream
({{ill|Sakaenoo Onsen|ja|栄之尾温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaenoo Onsen|qq=}}) 190714 Settuskyo Onsen Takatsuki Osaka pref Japan01s3.jpg|Open-air bath with a gorge view
({{ill|Settsukyō Onsen|ja|摂津峡温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Settsukyō Onsen|qq=}}) Alpa Komakusa 2008.jpg|Open-air bath with a lake view (Tazawako Kōgen Onsen “Alpa Komakusa”) Tamatsukuri onsen yado01s3648.jpg|A guest room with an attached open-air bath
([[:en:Tamatsukuri Onsen|Tamatsukuri Onsen]]) Koeda-Fudousan Katsura Koeda P6237036.JPG|A personal open-air bath built on private villa grounds == Management == [[File:新穂高の湯001.jpg|thumb|Cleaning an open-air bath
({{ill|Shinhodaka Onsen|ja|新穂高温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinhodaka Onsen|1=Shinhotaka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11503887}})]] From a management perspective, especially with a free-flowing hot spring of high source temperature, weather changes can significantly alter the bath’s temperature, requiring more precise temperature control. Additionally, due to the constant possibility of contamination by [[:en:Legionella|Legionella]] bacteria, stricter hygiene measures than those for indoor baths are neededJapan Association of Living and Environmental Health “[http://www.seiei.or.jp/db-cho/le-11.html Legionella Prevention Guidelines]” (Revised March 1999; accessed January 5, 2016).. == Other == [[File:Iceland - Blue Lagoon 09 (6571266721).jpg|thumb|[[:en:Blue Lagoon (geothermal spa)|Blue Lagoon]] ([[:en:Iceland|Iceland]])]] * The open-air bath “Edo-jōwa Yoichi” at [[:en:Spa Resort Hawaiians|Spa Resort Hawaiians]] in [[:en:Iwaki, Fukushima|Iwaki, Fukushima]], [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]] is listed in the “[[:en:Guinness World Records|Guinness World Records]] 2009” as the world’s largest open-air bath with a total tub area of 1000m²{{Cite web|和書|url=http://blog.hawaiians.co.jp/archives/50736632.html|title=世界一の大露天風呂|publisher=スパリゾートハワイアンズ|date=2008-11-08|accessdate=2021-06-03}}. * In [[:en:Iceland|Iceland]], there is an open-air spa called the [[:en:Blue Lagoon (geothermal spa)|Blue Lagoon]] that covers about 5,000m² (with approximately 2,400m² open for general bathing)Iceland Tourism & Culture Research Institute “[http://www.iceland-kankobunka.jp/guide/reykjanes/blgn1.htm Tourist Spot Guide: Blue Lagoon]” (accessed January 5, 2016).. * [[:en:June 26|June 26]] is “Open-Air Bath Day” (a pun on “Ro (6) Ten (.) Bu (2) Ro (6)”). It was established on June 26, 1987, by {{ill|Yubara Onsen|ja|湯原温泉|1=Yubara Onsen|2=ja|3=湯原温泉|4=ceb|5=Yubara hotspring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563486}} in [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]Conceived in 1987 as part of a local revitalization project by young people in Yubara Onsen.[http://www.net626.co.jp/roten/index.html Official site of the 6.26 Open-Air Bath Day Committee] (Yubara Town Ryokan Cooperative). == Notes == == Sources == == See Also == * {{ill|part_of voyeurism in open-air baths incidents|ja|露天風呂|1=Rotenburo|1=Rotenburoでの組織的盗撮事件|1=Organized voyeurism case in an open-air bath|3=露天風呂での組織的盗撮事件|1=Organized Voyeurism Scandal at Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of voyeurism in open-air baths incidents|13=User:Immanuelle/Rotenburo|qq=}} == External links == * [http://www.net626.co.jp/banduke.htm Open-Air Bath Banzuke Chart] {{Onsen-stub}} '''Rotenburo''' (露天風呂) {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|露天風呂|version=102106783|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:Bathing]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] }} [[ja:露天風呂]] [[ja:%E9%9C%B2%E5%A4%A9%E9%A2%A8%E5%91%82]] [[ko:%EB%85%B8%EC%B2%9C%ED%83%95]] [[Category:Hot_springs_of_Japan]] {{Commons category|Outdoor hot spring baths}} [[ko:노천탕]] {{wikidata link|Q10856214}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

[[commons:Category:Outdoor hot spring baths]][[ja:露天風呂]][[ko:노천탕]] [[commons:Category:Outdoor hot spring baths]][[ja:露天風呂]][[ko:노천탕]] [[sn:commons:Category:Outdoor hot spring baths]][[ja:露天風呂]][[ko:노천탕]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10856214]] Wikidata name: outdoor hot spring bath [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1={{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}} refers to communal baths outside lodging facilities shared by multiple users, {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} denotes in-house hot spring baths privately installed within lodging facilities, and {{ill|Rotenburo|ja|露天風呂|1=outdoor hot spring bath|1=Rotenburo|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rotenburo|13=User:Immanuelle/Rotenburo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10856214}} describes open-air baths typically without a roof or full enclosure.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:41, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Onsen in Nachikatsuura, Japan.jpg|thumb|280px|An open-air bath overlooking the sea ([[:en:Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen]])]] [[File:Iwojima Higashi onsen.jpg|280px|thumb|A self-flowing hot spring “{{ill|Higashi Onsen (Iōjima)|ja|東温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Onsen (Iōjima)|1=Higashi Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527997}}” facing the East China Sea ([[:en:Iōjima (Kagoshima)|Satsuma Iōjima]])]] '''Rotenburo''' (open-air bath, 露天風呂) is a type of [[:en:Furo|Bath]] built outdoors with no roof or enclosure[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%9C%B2%E5%A4%A9%E9%A2%A8%E5%91%82-663841 Kotobank - Digital Daijisen (Sanseidō)]. It is also called '''Noburo''' (野風呂) or '''Notenburo''' (野天風呂)When referred to as 野天風呂 (or 野風呂), it may mean a bath that is not entirely in its natural state like a {{ill|Wild hot spring|ja|野湯|1=Wild Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild Hot Springs|qq=}} but lacks as much development in walls or roofing as a typical open-air bath.. == Overview == Open-air baths can be considered the original “landscape” of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] bathing. They began as naturally emerging hot springs like {{ill|Wild hot spring|ja|野湯|1=Wild Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild Hot Springs|qq=}}, in which a simple bathing area (bathtub) was arranged. Rotenburo (open-air baths) are highly popular at hot spring resorts. Reasons include being able to enjoy scenic views while bathing, experiencing a sense of openness, and having less risk of overheating (since heat does not accumulate as much as in an indoor setting). As a result, many [[:en:Ryokan|Ryokan]] and {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} offer open-air baths in addition to {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}}. In recent years, not only traditional onsen but also many {{ill|Supersento|ja|スーパー銭湯|1=Super Sentō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Supersento|13=User:Immanuelle/Super Sentō|qq=}} have installed open-air baths. Up through the early 1980s, indoor styles such as {{ill|Roman bath|ja|ローマ風呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Roman bath|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579581}} or {{ill|Jungle bath|ja|ジャングル風呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jungle bath|qq=}} were mainstream. However, around the late 1980s during the [[:en:Japanese asset price bubble|Japanese asset price bubble]], rotenburo began to draw more attention. Sometimes, even a bath that has a ceiling and three walls (thus feeling nearly as enclosed as an indoor bath) might be marketed as a “rotenburo,” or described as “semi-open-air” (“半露天”). == Rotenburo Banzuke == '''Rotenburo Banzuke''' (露天風呂番付) is a form of {{ill|Onsen banzuke|ja|温泉番付|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen banzuke|1=onsen banzuke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11562960}}, Ranking hot spring areas that are famous for their open-air baths. It was compiled in 1981, edited by '''Noguchi Fuyuto'''. In this Ranking, the Western {{ill|Yokozuna|ja|横綱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokozuna|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=yokozuna|WD=Q1067277}} is the “Sunayu” at {{ill|Yubara Onsen|ja|湯原温泉|1=Yubara Onsen|2=ja|3=湯原温泉|4=ceb|5=Yubara hotspring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563486}}, and the Eastern Yokozuna is {{ill|Takaragawa Onsen|ja|宝川温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaragawa Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水上温泉郷|1=Minakami Onsenkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548229}}. At Yubara Onsen, there is a large monument depicting this Rotenburo Banzuke, serving as a symbol of the hot spring resort. == Variations of Open-Air Baths == Yumura onsen20n4592.jpg|Open-air bath in a forest setting
([[:en:Yumura Onsen (Hyogo)|Yumura Onsen]]) Ryokukeitouen Kirishima City Kagoshima Pref09n4592.jpg|Open-air bath at a mountain stream
({{ill|Sakaenoo Onsen|ja|栄之尾温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaenoo Onsen|qq=}}) 190714 Settuskyo Onsen Takatsuki Osaka pref Japan01s3.jpg|Open-air bath with a gorge view
({{ill|Settsukyō Onsen|ja|摂津峡温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Settsukyō Onsen|qq=}}) Alpa Komakusa 2008.jpg|Open-air bath with a lake view (Tazawako Kōgen Onsen “Alpa Komakusa”) Tamatsukuri onsen yado01s3648.jpg|A guest room with an attached open-air bath
([[:en:Tamatsukuri Onsen|Tamatsukuri Onsen]]) Koeda-Fudousan Katsura Koeda P6237036.JPG|A personal open-air bath built on private villa grounds == Management == [[File:新穂高の湯001.jpg|thumb|Cleaning an open-air bath
({{ill|Shinhodaka Onsen|ja|新穂高温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinhodaka Onsen|1=Shinhotaka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11503887}})]] From a management perspective, especially with a free-flowing hot spring of high source temperature, weather changes can significantly alter the bath’s temperature, requiring more precise temperature control. Additionally, due to the constant possibility of contamination by [[:en:Legionella|Legionella]] bacteria, stricter hygiene measures than those for indoor baths are neededJapan Association of Living and Environmental Health “[http://www.seiei.or.jp/db-cho/le-11.html Legionella Prevention Guidelines]” (Revised March 1999; accessed January 5, 2016).. == Other == [[File:Iceland - Blue Lagoon 09 (6571266721).jpg|thumb|[[:en:Blue Lagoon (geothermal spa)|Blue Lagoon]] ([[:en:Iceland|Iceland]])]] * The open-air bath “Edo-jōwa Yoichi” at [[:en:Spa Resort Hawaiians|Spa Resort Hawaiians]] in [[:en:Iwaki, Fukushima|Iwaki, Fukushima]], [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]] is listed in the “[[:en:Guinness World Records|Guinness World Records]] 2009” as the world’s largest open-air bath with a total tub area of 1000m²{{Cite web|和書|url=http://blog.hawaiians.co.jp/archives/50736632.html|title=世界一の大露天風呂|publisher=スパリゾートハワイアンズ|date=2008-11-08|accessdate=2021-06-03}}. * In [[:en:Iceland|Iceland]], there is an open-air spa called the [[:en:Blue Lagoon (geothermal spa)|Blue Lagoon]] that covers about 5,000m² (with approximately 2,400m² open for general bathing)Iceland Tourism & Culture Research Institute “[http://www.iceland-kankobunka.jp/guide/reykjanes/blgn1.htm Tourist Spot Guide: Blue Lagoon]” (accessed January 5, 2016).. * [[:en:June 26|June 26]] is “Open-Air Bath Day” (a pun on “Ro (6) Ten (.) Bu (2) Ro (6)”). It was established on June 26, 1987, by {{ill|Yubara Onsen|ja|湯原温泉|1=Yubara Onsen|2=ja|3=湯原温泉|4=ceb|5=Yubara hotspring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563486}} in [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]Conceived in 1987 as part of a local revitalization project by young people in Yubara Onsen.[http://www.net626.co.jp/roten/index.html Official site of the 6.26 Open-Air Bath Day Committee] (Yubara Town Ryokan Cooperative). == Notes == == Sources == == See Also == * {{ill|part_of voyeurism in open-air baths incidents|ja|露天風呂|1=Rotenburo|1=Rotenburoでの組織的盗撮事件|1=Organized voyeurism case in an open-air bath|3=露天風呂での組織的盗撮事件|1=Organized Voyeurism Scandal at Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of voyeurism in open-air baths incidents|13=User:Immanuelle/Rotenburo|qq=}} == External links == * [http://www.net626.co.jp/banduke.htm Open-Air Bath Banzuke Chart] {{Onsen-stub}} '''Rotenburo''' (露天風呂) {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|露天風呂|version=102106783|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[ja:露天風呂]] [[ja:%E9%9C%B2%E5%A4%A9%E9%A2%A8%E5%91%82]] [[ko:%EB%85%B8%EC%B2%9C%ED%83%95]] {{Commons category|Outdoor hot spring baths}} [[ko:노천탕]] {{wikidata link|Q10856214}} [[Category:Bathing]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Hot_springs_of_Japan]] [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[commons:Category:Outdoor hot spring baths]][[ja:露天風呂]][[ko:노천탕]] [[commons:Category:Outdoor hot spring baths]][[ja:露天風呂]][[ko:노천탕]] [[sn:commons:Category:Outdoor hot spring baths]][[ja:露天風呂]][[ko:노천탕]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10856214]] Wikidata name: outdoor hot spring bath [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1={{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}} refers to communal baths outside lodging facilities shared by multiple users, {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} denotes in-house hot spring baths privately installed within lodging facilities, and {{ill|Rotenburo|ja|露天風呂|1=outdoor hot spring bath|1=Rotenburo|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rotenburo|13=User:Immanuelle/Rotenburo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10856214}} describes open-air baths typically without a roof or full enclosure.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:41, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Onsen in Nachikatsuura, Japan.jpg|thumb|280px|An open-air bath overlooking the sea ([[:en:Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen]])]] [[File:Iwojima Higashi onsen.jpg|280px|thumb|A self-flowing hot spring “{{ill|Higashi Onsen (Iōjima)|ja|東温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Onsen (Iōjima)|1=Higashi Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527997}}” facing the East China Sea ([[:en:Iōjima (Kagoshima)|Satsuma Iōjima]])]] '''Rotenburo''' (open-air bath, 露天風呂) is a type of [[:en:Furo|Bath]] built outdoors with no roof or enclosure[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%9C%B2%E5%A4%A9%E9%A2%A8%E5%91%82-663841 Kotobank - Digital Daijisen (Sanseidō)]. It is also called '''Noburo''' (野風呂) or '''Notenburo''' (野天風呂)When referred to as 野天風呂 (or 野風呂), it may mean a bath that is not entirely in its natural state like a {{ill|Wild hot spring|ja|野湯|1=Wild Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild Hot Springs|qq=}} but lacks as much development in walls or roofing as a typical open-air bath.. == Overview == Open-air baths can be considered the original “landscape” of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] bathing. They began as naturally emerging hot springs like {{ill|Wild hot spring|ja|野湯|1=Wild Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild Hot Springs|qq=}}, in which a simple bathing area (bathtub) was arranged. Rotenburo (open-air baths) are highly popular at hot spring resorts. Reasons include being able to enjoy scenic views while bathing, experiencing a sense of openness, and having less risk of overheating (since heat does not accumulate as much as in an indoor setting). As a result, many [[:en:Ryokan|Ryokan]] and {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} offer open-air baths in addition to {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}}. In recent years, not only traditional onsen but also many {{ill|Supersento|ja|スーパー銭湯|1=Super Sentō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Supersento|13=User:Immanuelle/Super Sentō|qq=}} have installed open-air baths. Up through the early 1980s, indoor styles such as {{ill|Roman bath|ja|ローマ風呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Roman bath|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579581}} or {{ill|Jungle bath|ja|ジャングル風呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jungle bath|qq=}} were mainstream. However, around the late 1980s during the [[:en:Japanese asset price bubble|Japanese asset price bubble]], rotenburo began to draw more attention. Sometimes, even a bath that has a ceiling and three walls (thus feeling nearly as enclosed as an indoor bath) might be marketed as a “rotenburo,” or described as “semi-open-air” (“半露天”). == Rotenburo Banzuke == '''Rotenburo Banzuke''' (露天風呂番付) is a form of {{ill|Onsen banzuke|ja|温泉番付|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen banzuke|1=onsen banzuke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11562960}}, Ranking hot spring areas that are famous for their open-air baths. It was compiled in 1981, edited by '''Noguchi Fuyuto'''. In this Ranking, the Western {{ill|Yokozuna|ja|横綱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokozuna|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=yokozuna|WD=Q1067277}} is the “Sunayu” at {{ill|Yubara Onsen|ja|湯原温泉|1=Yubara Onsen|2=ja|3=湯原温泉|4=ceb|5=Yubara hotspring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563486}}, and the Eastern Yokozuna is {{ill|Takaragawa Onsen|ja|宝川温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaragawa Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水上温泉郷|1=Minakami Onsenkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548229}}. At Yubara Onsen, there is a large monument depicting this Rotenburo Banzuke, serving as a symbol of the hot spring resort. == Variations of Open-Air Baths == Yumura onsen20n4592.jpg|Open-air bath in a forest setting
([[:en:Yumura Onsen (Hyogo)|Yumura Onsen]]) Ryokukeitouen Kirishima City Kagoshima Pref09n4592.jpg|Open-air bath at a mountain stream
({{ill|Sakaenoo Onsen|ja|栄之尾温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaenoo Onsen|qq=}}) 190714 Settuskyo Onsen Takatsuki Osaka pref Japan01s3.jpg|Open-air bath with a gorge view
({{ill|Settsukyō Onsen|ja|摂津峡温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Settsukyō Onsen|qq=}}) Alpa Komakusa 2008.jpg|Open-air bath with a lake view (Tazawako Kōgen Onsen “Alpa Komakusa”) Tamatsukuri onsen yado01s3648.jpg|A guest room with an attached open-air bath
([[:en:Tamatsukuri Onsen|Tamatsukuri Onsen]]) Koeda-Fudousan Katsura Koeda P6237036.JPG|A personal open-air bath built on private villa grounds == Management == [[File:新穂高の湯001.jpg|thumb|Cleaning an open-air bath
({{ill|Shinhodaka Onsen|ja|新穂高温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinhodaka Onsen|1=Shinhotaka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11503887}})]] From a management perspective, especially with a free-flowing hot spring of high source temperature, weather changes can significantly alter the bath’s temperature, requiring more precise temperature control. Additionally, due to the constant possibility of contamination by [[:en:Legionella|Legionella]] bacteria, stricter hygiene measures than those for indoor baths are neededJapan Association of Living and Environmental Health “[http://www.seiei.or.jp/db-cho/le-11.html Legionella Prevention Guidelines]” (Revised March 1999; accessed January 5, 2016).. == Other == [[File:Iceland - Blue Lagoon 09 (6571266721).jpg|thumb|[[:en:Blue Lagoon (geothermal spa)|Blue Lagoon]] ([[:en:Iceland|Iceland]])]] * The open-air bath “Edo-jōwa Yoichi” at [[:en:Spa Resort Hawaiians|Spa Resort Hawaiians]] in [[:en:Iwaki, Fukushima|Iwaki, Fukushima]], [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]] is listed in the “[[:en:Guinness World Records|Guinness World Records]] 2009” as the world’s largest open-air bath with a total tub area of 1000m²{{Cite web|和書|url=http://blog.hawaiians.co.jp/archives/50736632.html|title=世界一の大露天風呂|publisher=スパリゾートハワイアンズ|date=2008-11-08|accessdate=2021-06-03}}. * In [[:en:Iceland|Iceland]], there is an open-air spa called the [[:en:Blue Lagoon (geothermal spa)|Blue Lagoon]] that covers about 5,000m² (with approximately 2,400m² open for general bathing)Iceland Tourism & Culture Research Institute “[http://www.iceland-kankobunka.jp/guide/reykjanes/blgn1.htm Tourist Spot Guide: Blue Lagoon]” (accessed January 5, 2016).. * [[:en:June 26|June 26]] is “Open-Air Bath Day” (a pun on “Ro (6) Ten (.) Bu (2) Ro (6)”). It was established on June 26, 1987, by {{ill|Yubara Onsen|ja|湯原温泉|1=Yubara Onsen|2=ja|3=湯原温泉|4=ceb|5=Yubara hotspring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563486}} in [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]Conceived in 1987 as part of a local revitalization project by young people in Yubara Onsen.[http://www.net626.co.jp/roten/index.html Official site of the 6.26 Open-Air Bath Day Committee] (Yubara Town Ryokan Cooperative). == Notes == == Sources == == See Also == * {{ill|part_of voyeurism in open-air baths incidents|ja|露天風呂|1=Rotenburo|1=Rotenburoでの組織的盗撮事件|1=Organized voyeurism case in an open-air bath|3=露天風呂での組織的盗撮事件|1=Organized Voyeurism Scandal at Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of voyeurism in open-air baths incidents|13=User:Immanuelle/Rotenburo|qq=}} == External links == * [http://www.net626.co.jp/banduke.htm Open-Air Bath Banzuke Chart] {{Onsen-stub}} '''Rotenburo''' (露天風呂) {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|露天風呂|version=102106783|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| }} [[ja:露天風呂]] [[ja:%E9%9C%B2%E5%A4%A9%E9%A2%A8%E5%91%82]] [[ko:%EB%85%B8%EC%B2%9C%ED%83%95]] {{Commons category|Outdoor hot spring baths}} [[ko:노천탕]] {{wikidata link|Q10856214}} [[Category:Bathing]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Hot_springs_of_Japan]] [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

It was established on June 26, 1987, by {{ill|Yubara Onsen|ja|湯原温泉|1=Yubara Onsen|2=ja|3=湯原温泉|4=ceb|5=Yubara hotspring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563486}} in [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]Conceived in 1987 as part of a local revitalization project by young people in Yubara Onsen.[http://www.net626.co.jp/roten/index.html Official site of the 6.26 Open-Air Bath Day Committee] (Yubara Town Ryokan Cooperative). == Notes == == Sources == == See Also == * {{ill|part_of voyeurism in open-air baths incidents|ja|露天風呂|1=Rotenburo|1=Rotenburoでの組織的盗撮事件|1=Organized voyeurism case in an open-air bath|3=露天風呂での組織的盗撮事件|1=Organized Voyeurism Scandal at Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of voyeurism in open-air baths incidents|13=User:Immanuelle/Rotenburo|qq=}} == External links == * [http://www.net626.co.jp/banduke.htm Open-Air Bath Banzuke Chart] {{Onsen-stub}} '''Rotenburo''' (露天風呂) {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|露天風呂|version=102106783|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:露天風呂]] [[ja:%E9%9C%B2%E5%A4%A9%E9%A2%A8%E5%91%82]] [[ko:%EB%85%B8%EC%B2%9C%ED%83%95]] {{Commons category|Outdoor hot spring baths}} [[ko:노천탕]] {{wikidata link|Q10856214}} [[Category:Bathing]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Hot_springs_of_Japan]] [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[co:commons:Category:Outdoor hot spring baths]][[ja:露天風呂]][[ko:노천탕]] [[co:commons:Category:Outdoor hot spring baths]][[ja:露天風呂]][[ko:노천탕]] [[sn:commons:Category:Outdoor hot spring baths]][[ja:露天風呂]][[ko:노천탕]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10856214]] Wikidata name: outdoor hot spring bath [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1={{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}} refers to communal baths outside lodging facilities shared by multiple users, {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} denotes in-house hot spring baths privately installed within lodging facilities, and {{ill|Rotenburo|ja|露天風呂|1=outdoor hot spring bath|1=Rotenburo|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rotenburo|13=User:Immanuelle/Rotenburo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10856214}} describes open-air baths typically without a roof or full enclosure.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:41, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Onsen in Nachikatsuura, Japan.jpg|thumb|280px|An open-air bath overlooking the sea ([[:en:Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen]])]] [[File:Iwojima Higashi onsen.jpg|280px|thumb|A self-flowing hot spring “{{ill|Higashi Onsen (Iōjima)|ja|東温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Onsen (Iōjima)|1=Higashi Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527997}}” facing the East China Sea ([[:en:Iōjima (Kagoshima)|Satsuma Iōjima]])]] '''Rotenburo''' (open-air bath, 露天風呂) is a type of [[:en:Furo|Bath]] built outdoors with no roof or enclosure[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%9C%B2%E5%A4%A9%E9%A2%A8%E5%91%82-663841 Kotobank - Digital Daijisen (Sanseidō)]. It is also called '''Noburo''' (野風呂) or '''Notenburo''' (野天風呂)When referred to as 野天風呂 (or 野風呂), it may mean a bath that is not entirely in its natural state like a {{ill|Wild hot spring|ja|野湯|1=Wild Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild Hot Springs|qq=}} but lacks as much development in walls or roofing as a typical open-air bath.. == Overview == Open-air baths can be considered the original “landscape” of [[:en:Onsen|Onsen]] bathing. They began as naturally emerging hot springs like {{ill|Wild hot spring|ja|野湯|1=Wild Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild Hot Springs|qq=}}, in which a simple bathing area (bathtub) was arranged. Rotenburo (open-air baths) are highly popular at hot spring resorts. Reasons include being able to enjoy scenic views while bathing, experiencing a sense of openness, and having less risk of overheating (since heat does not accumulate as much as in an indoor setting). As a result, many [[:en:Ryokan|Ryokan]] and {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} offer open-air baths in addition to {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}}. In recent years, not only traditional onsen but also many {{ill|Supersento|ja|スーパー銭湯|1=Super Sentō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Supersento|13=User:Immanuelle/Super Sentō|qq=}} have installed open-air baths. Up through the early 1980s, indoor styles such as {{ill|Roman bath|ja|ローマ風呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Roman bath|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579581}} or {{ill|Jungle bath|ja|ジャングル風呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jungle bath|qq=}} were mainstream. However, around the late 1980s during the [[:en:Japanese asset price bubble|Japanese asset price bubble]], rotenburo began to draw more attention. Sometimes, even a bath that has a ceiling and three walls (thus feeling nearly as enclosed as an indoor bath) might be marketed as a “rotenburo,” or described as “semi-open-air” (“半露天”). == Rotenburo Banzuke == '''Rotenburo Banzuke''' (露天風呂番付) is a form of {{ill|Onsen banzuke|ja|温泉番付|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen banzuke|1=onsen banzuke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11562960}}, Ranking hot spring areas that are famous for their open-air baths. It was compiled in 1981, edited by '''Noguchi Fuyuto'''. In this Ranking, the Western {{ill|Yokozuna|ja|横綱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokozuna|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=yokozuna|WD=Q1067277}} is the “Sunayu” at {{ill|Yubara Onsen|ja|湯原温泉|1=Yubara Onsen|2=ja|3=湯原温泉|4=ceb|5=Yubara hotspring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563486}}, and the Eastern Yokozuna is {{ill|Takaragawa Onsen|ja|宝川温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaragawa Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水上温泉郷|1=Minakami Onsenkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548229}}. At Yubara Onsen, there is a large monument depicting this Rotenburo Banzuke, serving as a symbol of the hot spring resort. == Variations of Open-Air Baths == Yumura onsen20n4592.jpg|Open-air bath in a forest setting
([[:en:Yumura Onsen (Hyogo)|Yumura Onsen]]) Ryokukeitouen Kirishima City Kagoshima Pref09n4592.jpg|Open-air bath at a mountain stream
({{ill|Sakaenoo Onsen|ja|栄之尾温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaenoo Onsen|qq=}}) 190714 Settuskyo Onsen Takatsuki Osaka pref Japan01s3.jpg|Open-air bath with a gorge view
({{ill|Settsukyō Onsen|ja|摂津峡温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Settsukyō Onsen|qq=}}) Alpa Komakusa 2008.jpg|Open-air bath with a lake view (Tazawako Kōgen Onsen “Alpa Komakusa”) Tamatsukuri onsen yado01s3648.jpg|A guest room with an attached open-air bath
([[:en:Tamatsukuri Onsen|Tamatsukuri Onsen]]) Koeda-Fudousan Katsura Koeda P6237036.JPG|A personal open-air bath built on private villa grounds == Management == [[File:新穂高の湯001.jpg|thumb|Cleaning an open-air bath
({{ill|Shinhodaka Onsen|ja|新穂高温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinhodaka Onsen|1=Shinhotaka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11503887}})]] From a management perspective, especially with a free-flowing hot spring of high source temperature, weather changes can significantly alter the bath’s temperature, requiring more precise temperature control. Additionally, due to the constant possibility of contamination by [[:en:Legionella|Legionella]] bacteria, stricter hygiene measures than those for indoor baths are neededJapan Association of Living and Environmental Health “[http://www.seiei.or.jp/db-cho/le-11.html Legionella Prevention Guidelines]” (Revised March 1999; accessed January 5, 2016).. == Other == [[File:Iceland - Blue Lagoon 09 (6571266721).jpg|thumb|[[:en:Blue Lagoon (geothermal spa)|Blue Lagoon]] ([[:en:Iceland|Iceland]])]] * The open-air bath “Edo-jōwa Yoichi” at [[:en:Spa Resort Hawaiians|Spa Resort Hawaiians]] in [[:en:Iwaki, Fukushima|Iwaki, Fukushima]], [[:en:Fukushima Prefecture|Fukushima Prefecture]] is listed in the “[[:en:Guinness World Records|Guinness World Records]] 2009” as the world’s largest open-air bath with a total tub area of 1000m²{{Cite web|和書|url=http://blog.hawaiians.co.jp/archives/50736632.html|title=世界一の大露天風呂|publisher=スパリゾートハワイアンズ|date=2008-11-08|accessdate=2021-06-03}}. * In [[:en:Iceland|Iceland]], there is an open-air spa called the [[:en:Blue Lagoon (geothermal spa)|Blue Lagoon]] that covers about 5,000m² (with approximately 2,400m² open for general bathing)Iceland Tourism & Culture Research Institute “[http://www.iceland-kankobunka.jp/guide/reykjanes/blgn1.htm Tourist Spot Guide: Blue Lagoon]” (accessed January 5, 2016).. * [[:en:June 26|June 26]] is “Open-Air Bath Day” (a pun on “Ro (6) Ten (.) Bu (2) Ro (6)”). It was established on June 26, 1987, by {{ill|Yubara Onsen|ja|湯原温泉|1=Yubara Onsen|2=ja|3=湯原温泉|4=ceb|5=Yubara hotspring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563486}} in [[:en:Okayama Prefecture|Okayama Prefecture]]Conceived in 1987 as part of a local revitalization project by young people in Yubara Onsen.[http://www.net626.co.jp/roten/index.html Official site of the 6.26 Open-Air Bath Day Committee] (Yubara Town Ryokan Cooperative). == Notes == == Sources == == See Also == * {{ill|part_of voyeurism in open-air baths incidents|ja|露天風呂|1=Rotenburo|1=Rotenburoでの組織的盗撮事件|1=Organized voyeurism case in an open-air bath|3=露天風呂での組織的盗撮事件|1=Organized Voyeurism Scandal at Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of voyeurism in open-air baths incidents|13=User:Immanuelle/Rotenburo|qq=}} == External links == * [http://www.net626.co.jp/banduke.htm Open-Air Bath Banzuke Chart] {{Onsen-stub}} '''Rotenburo''' (露天風呂) {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|露天風呂|version=102106783|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:露天風呂]] [[ja:%E9%9C%B2%E5%A4%A9%E9%A2%A8%E5%91%82]] [[ko:%EB%85%B8%EC%B2%9C%ED%83%95]] {{Commons category|Outdoor hot spring baths}} [[ko:노천탕]] {{wikidata link|Q10856214}} [[Category:Bathing]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Hot_springs_of_Japan]] [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

It is also called '''Noburo''' (野風呂) or '''Notenburo''' (野天風呂)When referred to as 野天風呂 (or 野風呂), it may mean a bath that is not entirely in its natural state like a {{ill|Wild hot spring|ja|野湯|1=Wild Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild Hot Springs|qq=}} but lacks as much development in walls or roofing as a typical open-air bath.. == Overview == Open-air baths can be considered the original “landscape” of {{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|WD=Q655311|lt=Onsen}} bathing. They began as naturally emerging hot springs like {{ill|Wild hot spring|ja|野湯|1=Wild Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild Hot Springs|qq=}}, in which a simple bathing area (bathtub) was arranged. Rotenburo (open-air baths) are highly popular at hot spring resorts. Reasons include being able to enjoy scenic views while bathing, experiencing a sense of openness, and having less risk of overheating (since heat does not accumulate as much as in an indoor setting). As a result, many {{ill|Ryokan|en|Ryokan|WD=Q562930|lt=Ryokan}} and {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} offer open-air baths in addition to {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}}. In recent years, not only traditional onsen but also many {{ill|Supersento|ja|スーパー銭湯|1=Super Sentō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Supersento|13=User:Immanuelle/Super Sentō|qq=}} have installed open-air baths. Up through the early 1980s, indoor styles such as {{ill|Roman bath|ja|ローマ風呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Roman bath|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579581}} or {{ill|Jungle bath|ja|ジャングル風呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jungle bath|qq=}} were mainstream. However, around the late 1980s during the {{ill|Japanese asset price bubble|en|Japanese asset price bubble|WD=Q6500827|lt=Japanese asset price bubble}}, rotenburo began to draw more attention. Sometimes, even a bath that has a ceiling and three walls (thus feeling nearly as enclosed as an indoor bath) might be marketed as a “rotenburo,” or described as “semi-open-air” (“半露天”). == Rotenburo Banzuke == '''Rotenburo Banzuke''' (露天風呂番付) is a form of {{ill|Onsen banzuke|ja|温泉番付|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen banzuke|1=onsen banzuke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11562960}}, Ranking hot spring areas that are famous for their open-air baths. It was compiled in 1981, edited by '''Noguchi Fuyuto'''. In this Ranking, the Western {{ill|Yokozuna|ja|横綱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokozuna|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=yokozuna|WD=Q1067277}} is the “Sunayu” at {{ill|Yubara Onsen|ja|湯原温泉|1=Yubara Onsen|2=ja|3=湯原温泉|4=ceb|5=Yubara hotspring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563486}}, and the Eastern Yokozuna is {{ill|Takaragawa Onsen|ja|宝川温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaragawa Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水上温泉郷|1=Minakami Onsenkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548229}}. At Yubara Onsen, there is a large monument depicting this Rotenburo Banzuke, serving as a symbol of the hot spring resort. == Variations of Open-Air Baths == Yumura onsen20n4592.jpg|Open-air bath in a forest setting
({{ill|Yumura Onsen (Hyogo)|en|Yumura Onsen (Hyogo)|WD=Q8061163|lt=Yumura Onsen}}) Ryokukeitouen Kirishima City Kagoshima Pref09n4592.jpg|Open-air bath at a mountain stream
({{ill|Sakaenoo Onsen|ja|栄之尾温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaenoo Onsen|qq=}}) 190714 Settuskyo Onsen Takatsuki Osaka pref Japan01s3.jpg|Open-air bath with a gorge view
({{ill|Settsukyō Onsen|ja|摂津峡温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Settsukyō Onsen|qq=}}) Alpa Komakusa 2008.jpg|Open-air bath with a lake view (Tazawako Kōgen Onsen “Alpa Komakusa”) Tamatsukuri onsen yado01s3648.jpg|A guest room with an attached open-air bath
({{ill|Tamatsukuri Onsen|en|Tamatsukuri Onsen|WD=Q3514711|lt=Tamatsukuri Onsen}}) Koeda-Fudousan Katsura Koeda P6237036.JPG|A personal open-air bath built on private villa grounds == Management == [[File:新穂高の湯001.jpg|thumb|Cleaning an open-air bath
({{ill|Shinhodaka Onsen|ja|新穂高温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinhodaka Onsen|1=Shinhotaka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11503887}})]] From a management perspective, especially with a free-flowing hot spring of high source temperature, weather changes can significantly alter the bath’s temperature, requiring more precise temperature control. Additionally, due to the constant possibility of contamination by {{ill|Legionella|en|Legionella|WD=Q158013|lt=Legionella}} bacteria, stricter hygiene measures than those for indoor baths are neededJapan Association of Living and Environmental Health “[http://www.seiei.or.jp/db-cho/le-11.html Legionella Prevention Guidelines]” (Revised March 1999; accessed January 5, 2016).. == Other == [[File:Iceland - Blue Lagoon 09 (6571266721).jpg|thumb|{{ill|Blue Lagoon (geothermal spa)|en|Blue Lagoon (geothermal spa)|WD=Q886946|lt=Blue Lagoon}} ({{ill|Iceland|en|Iceland|WD=Q189|lt=Iceland}})]] * The open-air bath “Edo-jōwa Yoichi” at {{ill|Spa Resort Hawaiians|en|Spa Resort Hawaiians|WD=Q7572157|lt=Spa Resort Hawaiians}} in {{ill|Iwaki, Fukushima|en|Iwaki, Fukushima|WD=Q274384|lt=Iwaki, Fukushima}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|en|Fukushima Prefecture|WD=Q71707|lt=Fukushima Prefecture}} is listed in the “{{ill|Guinness World Records|en|Guinness World Records|WD=Q41675|lt=Guinness World Records}} 2009” as the world’s largest open-air bath with a total tub area of 1000m²{{Cite web|和書|url=http://blog.hawaiians.co.jp/archives/50736632.html|title=世界一の大露天風呂|publisher=スパリゾートハワイアンズ|date=2008-11-08|accessdate=2021-06-03}}. * In {{ill|Iceland|en|Iceland|WD=Q189|lt=Iceland}}, there is an open-air spa called the {{ill|Blue Lagoon (geothermal spa)|en|Blue Lagoon (geothermal spa)|WD=Q886946|lt=Blue Lagoon}} that covers about 5,000m² (with approximately 2,400m² open for general bathing)Iceland Tourism & Culture Research Institute “[http://www.iceland-kankobunka.jp/guide/reykjanes/blgn1.htm Tourist Spot Guide: Blue Lagoon]” (accessed January 5, 2016).. * {{ill|June 26|en|June 26|WD=Q2661|lt=June 26}} is “Open-Air Bath Day” (a pun on “Ro (6) Ten (.) Bu (2) Ro (6)”). It was established on June 26, 1987, by {{ill|Yubara Onsen|ja|湯原温泉|1=Yubara Onsen|2=ja|3=湯原温泉|4=ceb|5=Yubara hotspring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563486}} in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}Conceived in 1987 as part of a local revitalization project by young people in Yubara Onsen.[http://www.net626.co.jp/roten/index.html Official site of the 6.26 Open-Air Bath Day Committee] (Yubara Town Ryokan Cooperative). == Notes == == Sources == == See Also == * {{ill|part_of voyeurism in open-air baths incidents|ja|露天風呂|1=Rotenburo|1=Rotenburoでの組織的盗撮事件|1=Organized voyeurism case in an open-air bath|3=露天風呂での組織的盗撮事件|1=Organized Voyeurism Scandal at Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of voyeurism in open-air baths incidents|13=User:Immanuelle/Rotenburo|qq=}} == External links == * [http://www.net626.co.jp/banduke.htm Open-Air Bath Banzuke Chart] {{Onsen-stub}} '''Rotenburo''' (露天風呂) {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|露天風呂|version=102106783|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:露天風呂]] [[ja:%E9%9C%B2%E5%A4%A9%E9%A2%A8%E5%91%82]] [[ko:%EB%85%B8%EC%B2%9C%ED%83%95]] {{Commons category|Outdoor hot spring baths}} [[ko:노천탕]] {{wikidata link|Q10856214}} [[Category:Bathing]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Hot_springs_of_Japan]] [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

revision.text:
[[co:commons:Category:Outdoor hot spring baths]][[ja:露天風呂]][[ko:노천탕]] [[co:commons:Category:Outdoor hot spring baths]][[ja:露天風呂]][[ko:노천탕]] [[sn:commons:Category:Outdoor hot spring baths]][[ja:露天風呂]][[ko:노천탕]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q10856214]] Wikidata name: outdoor hot spring bath [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:51, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1={{ill|Sotoyu|ja|外湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sotoyu|qq=}} refers to communal baths outside lodging facilities shared by multiple users, {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}} denotes in-house hot spring baths privately installed within lodging facilities, and {{ill|Rotenburo|ja|露天風呂|1=outdoor hot spring bath|1=Rotenburo|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rotenburo|13=User:Immanuelle/Rotenburo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10856214}} describes open-air baths typically without a roof or full enclosure.[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 01:41, 25 March 2025 (UTC)}} [[File:Onsen in Nachikatsuura, Japan.jpg|thumb|280px|An open-air bath overlooking the sea ({{ill|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|en|Nanki-Katsuura Onsen|WD=Q11408343|lt=Nanki-Katsuura Onsen}})]] [[File:Iwojima Higashi onsen.jpg|280px|thumb|A self-flowing hot spring “{{ill|Higashi Onsen (Iōjima)|ja|東温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Onsen (Iōjima)|1=Higashi Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11527997}}” facing the East China Sea ({{ill|Iōjima (Kagoshima)|en|Iōjima (Kagoshima)|WD=Q705894|lt=Satsuma Iōjima}})]] '''Rotenburo''' (open-air bath, 露天風呂) is a type of {{ill|Furo|en|Furo|WD=Q2637646|lt=Bath}} built outdoors with no roof or enclosure[https://kotobank.jp/word/%E9%9C%B2%E5%A4%A9%E9%A2%A8%E5%91%82-663841 Kotobank - Digital Daijisen (Sanseidō)]. It is also called '''Noburo''' (野風呂) or '''Notenburo''' (野天風呂)When referred to as 野天風呂 (or 野風呂), it may mean a bath that is not entirely in its natural state like a {{ill|Wild hot spring|ja|野湯|1=Wild Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild Hot Springs|qq=}} but lacks as much development in walls or roofing as a typical open-air bath.. == Overview == Open-air baths can be considered the original “landscape” of {{ill|Onsen|en|Onsen|WD=Q655311|lt=Onsen}} bathing. They began as naturally emerging hot springs like {{ill|Wild hot spring|ja|野湯|1=Wild Hot Springs|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild hot spring|13=User:Immanuelle/Wild Hot Springs|qq=}}, in which a simple bathing area (bathtub) was arranged. Rotenburo (open-air baths) are highly popular at hot spring resorts. Reasons include being able to enjoy scenic views while bathing, experiencing a sense of openness, and having less risk of overheating (since heat does not accumulate as much as in an indoor setting). As a result, many {{ill|Ryokan|en|Ryokan|WD=Q562930|lt=Ryokan}} and {{ill|Day-use hot spring facility|ja|日帰り入浴施設|1=1-day onsen facility|1=1-day onsen facility|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Day-use hot spring facility|13=User:Immanuelle/1-day onsen facility|qq=}} offer open-air baths in addition to {{ill|Uchiyu|ja|内湯|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uchiyu|qq=}}. In recent years, not only traditional onsen but also many {{ill|Supersento|ja|スーパー銭湯|1=Super Sentō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Supersento|13=User:Immanuelle/Super Sentō|qq=}} have installed open-air baths. Up through the early 1980s, indoor styles such as {{ill|Roman bath|ja|ローマ風呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Roman bath|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135579581}} or {{ill|Jungle bath|ja|ジャングル風呂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jungle bath|qq=}} were mainstream. However, around the late 1980s during the {{ill|Japanese asset price bubble|en|Japanese asset price bubble|WD=Q6500827|lt=Japanese asset price bubble}}, rotenburo began to draw more attention. Sometimes, even a bath that has a ceiling and three walls (thus feeling nearly as enclosed as an indoor bath) might be marketed as a “rotenburo,” or described as “semi-open-air” (“半露天”). == Rotenburo Banzuke == '''Rotenburo Banzuke''' (露天風呂番付) is a form of {{ill|Onsen banzuke|ja|温泉番付|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Onsen banzuke|1=onsen banzuke|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11562960}}, Ranking hot spring areas that are famous for their open-air baths. It was compiled in 1981, edited by '''Noguchi Fuyuto'''. In this Ranking, the Western {{ill|Yokozuna|ja|横綱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yokozuna|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=yokozuna|WD=Q1067277}} is the “Sunayu” at {{ill|Yubara Onsen|ja|湯原温泉|1=Yubara Onsen|2=ja|3=湯原温泉|4=ceb|5=Yubara hotspring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563486}}, and the Eastern Yokozuna is {{ill|Takaragawa Onsen|ja|宝川温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takaragawa Onsen|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 水上温泉郷|1=Minakami Onsenkyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11548229}}. At Yubara Onsen, there is a large monument depicting this Rotenburo Banzuke, serving as a symbol of the hot spring resort. == Variations of Open-Air Baths == Yumura onsen20n4592.jpg|Open-air bath in a forest setting
({{ill|Yumura Onsen (Hyogo)|en|Yumura Onsen (Hyogo)|WD=Q8061163|lt=Yumura Onsen}}) Ryokukeitouen Kirishima City Kagoshima Pref09n4592.jpg|Open-air bath at a mountain stream
({{ill|Sakaenoo Onsen|ja|栄之尾温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaenoo Onsen|qq=}}) 190714 Settuskyo Onsen Takatsuki Osaka pref Japan01s3.jpg|Open-air bath with a gorge view
({{ill|Settsukyō Onsen|ja|摂津峡温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Settsukyō Onsen|qq=}}) Alpa Komakusa 2008.jpg|Open-air bath with a lake view (Tazawako Kōgen Onsen “Alpa Komakusa”) Tamatsukuri onsen yado01s3648.jpg|A guest room with an attached open-air bath
({{ill|Tamatsukuri Onsen|en|Tamatsukuri Onsen|WD=Q3514711|lt=Tamatsukuri Onsen}}) Koeda-Fudousan Katsura Koeda P6237036.JPG|A personal open-air bath built on private villa grounds == Management == [[File:新穂高の湯001.jpg|thumb|Cleaning an open-air bath
({{ill|Shinhodaka Onsen|ja|新穂高温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinhodaka Onsen|1=Shinhotaka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11503887}})]] From a management perspective, especially with a free-flowing hot spring of high source temperature, weather changes can significantly alter the bath’s temperature, requiring more precise temperature control. Additionally, due to the constant possibility of contamination by {{ill|Legionella|en|Legionella|WD=Q158013|lt=Legionella}} bacteria, stricter hygiene measures than those for indoor baths are neededJapan Association of Living and Environmental Health “[http://www.seiei.or.jp/db-cho/le-11.html Legionella Prevention Guidelines]” (Revised March 1999; accessed January 5, 2016).. == Other == [[File:Iceland - Blue Lagoon 09 (6571266721).jpg|thumb|{{ill|Blue Lagoon (geothermal spa)|en|Blue Lagoon (geothermal spa)|WD=Q886946|lt=Blue Lagoon}} ({{ill|Iceland|en|Iceland|WD=Q189|lt=Iceland}})]] * The open-air bath “Edo-jōwa Yoichi” at {{ill|Spa Resort Hawaiians|en|Spa Resort Hawaiians|WD=Q7572157|lt=Spa Resort Hawaiians}} in {{ill|Iwaki, Fukushima|en|Iwaki, Fukushima|WD=Q274384|lt=Iwaki, Fukushima}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|en|Fukushima Prefecture|WD=Q71707|lt=Fukushima Prefecture}} is listed in the “{{ill|Guinness World Records|en|Guinness World Records|WD=Q41675|lt=Guinness World Records}} 2009” as the world’s largest open-air bath with a total tub area of 1000m²{{Cite web|和書|url=http://blog.hawaiians.co.jp/archives/50736632.html|title=世界一の大露天風呂|publisher=スパリゾートハワイアンズ|date=2008-11-08|accessdate=2021-06-03}}. * In {{ill|Iceland|en|Iceland|WD=Q189|lt=Iceland}}, there is an open-air spa called the {{ill|Blue Lagoon (geothermal spa)|en|Blue Lagoon (geothermal spa)|WD=Q886946|lt=Blue Lagoon}} that covers about 5,000m² (with approximately 2,400m² open for general bathing)Iceland Tourism & Culture Research Institute “[http://www.iceland-kankobunka.jp/guide/reykjanes/blgn1.htm Tourist Spot Guide: Blue Lagoon]” (accessed January 5, 2016).. * {{ill|June 26|en|June 26|WD=Q2661|lt=June 26}} is “Open-Air Bath Day” (a pun on “Ro (6) Ten (.) Bu (2) Ro (6)”). It was established on June 26, 1987, by {{ill|Yubara Onsen|ja|湯原温泉|1=Yubara Onsen|2=ja|3=湯原温泉|4=ceb|5=Yubara hotspring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563486}} in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}Conceived in 1987 as part of a local revitalization project by young people in Yubara Onsen.[http://www.net626.co.jp/roten/index.html Official site of the 6.26 Open-Air Bath Day Committee] (Yubara Town Ryokan Cooperative). == Notes == == Sources == == See Also == * {{ill|part_of voyeurism in open-air baths incidents|ja|露天風呂|1=Rotenburo|1=Rotenburoでの組織的盗撮事件|1=Organized voyeurism case in an open-air bath|3=露天風呂での組織的盗撮事件|1=Organized Voyeurism Scandal at Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of voyeurism in open-air baths incidents|13=User:Immanuelle/Rotenburo|qq=}} == External links == * [http://www.net626.co.jp/banduke.htm Open-Air Bath Banzuke Chart] {{Onsen-stub}} '''Rotenburo''' (露天風呂) {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|露天風呂|version=102106783|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:露天風呂]] [[ja:%E9%9C%B2%E5%A4%A9%E9%A2%A8%E5%91%82]] [[ko:%EB%85%B8%EC%B2%9C%ED%83%95]] {{Commons category|Outdoor hot spring baths}} [[ko:노천탕]] {{wikidata link|Q10856214}} [[Category:Bathing]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Hot_springs_of_Japan]] [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

However, around the late 1980s during the {{ill|Japanese asset price bubble|en|Japanese asset price bubble|WD=Q6500827|lt=Japanese asset price bubble}}, rotenburo began to draw more attention. ({{ill|Sakaenoo Onsen|ja|栄之尾温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakaenoo Onsen|qq=}}) 190714 Settuskyo Onsen Takatsuki Osaka pref Japan01s3.jpg|Open-air bath with a gorge view
({{ill|Settsukyō Onsen|ja|摂津峡温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Settsukyō Onsen|qq=}}) Alpa Komakusa 2008.jpg|Open-air bath with a lake view (Tazawako Kōgen Onsen “Alpa Komakusa”) Tamatsukuri onsen yado01s3648.jpg|A guest room with an attached open-air bath

({{ill|Shinhodaka Onsen|ja|新穂高温泉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinhodaka Onsen|1=Shinhotaka Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11503887}})]] From a management perspective, especially with a free-flowing hot spring of high source temperature, weather changes can significantly alter the bath’s temperature, requiring more precise temperature control. Bu (2) Ro (6)”). It was established on June 26, 1987, by {{ill|Yubara Onsen|ja|湯原温泉|1=Yubara Onsen|2=ja|3=湯原温泉|4=ceb|5=Yubara hotspring|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|13=User:Immanuelle/Yubara Onsen|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11563486}} in {{ill|Okayama Prefecture|en|Okayama Prefecture|WD=Q132936|lt=Okayama Prefecture}}Conceived in 1987 as part of a local revitalization project by young people in Yubara Onsen.[http://www.net626.co.jp/roten/index.html Official site of the 6.26 Open-Air Bath Day Committee] (Yubara Town Ryokan Cooperative). == Notes == == Sources == == See Also == * {{ill|part_of voyeurism in open-air baths incidents|ja|露天風呂|1=Rotenburo|1=Rotenburoでの組織的盗撮事件|1=Organized voyeurism case in an open-air bath|3=露天風呂での組織的盗撮事件|1=Organized Voyeurism Scandal at Outdoor hot spring baths|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/part_of voyeurism in open-air baths incidents|13=User:Immanuelle/Rotenburo|qq=}} == External links == * [http://www.net626.co.jp/banduke.htm Open-Air Bath Banzuke Chart] {{Onsen-stub}} '''Rotenburo''' (露天風呂) {{Onsen}} {{translated page|ja|露天風呂|version=102106783|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:露天風呂]] [[ja:%E9%9C%B2%E5%A4%A9%E9%A2%A8%E5%91%82]] [[ko:%EB%85%B8%EC%B2%9C%ED%83%95]] {{Commons category|Outdoor hot spring baths}} [[ko:노천탕]] {{wikidata link|Q10856214}} [[Category:Bathing]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Hot_springs_of_Japan]] [[Category:Onsens]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
